data.title: SFL Corporation: Juggling Debt, Struggling Dividend
data.description:
SFL Corporation (SFL) is a maritime shipper that offers a dividend over 9%. The yield is likely priced as such because of weaknesses in the balance sheet. The threat of further cuts looms, and investors should seek a better discount before buying.

Summary of Original Thesis

When I wrote about SFL last July, I described its then-yield of 11% as attractive but vulnerable to cuts.

A maritime shipper, SFL was exposed to macro cycles, and these had materialized over the years with falls and rises in its annual dividend.

On a forward-looking basis, SFL's problem was significant debt maturities in each approaching year. Annual free cash flow over the past decade was generally insufficient to repay this. With long-term leases on their shipping business as well, this made it difficult for them to raise prices and increase cash flow, putting them in a tight spot.

I therefore believed dividend cuts were likely unless they could liquidate some assets to reduce their debt burden. I couldn't see the reason for a Buy rating, in spite of the high yield.

Subsequent Updates and Financials

The first matter to assess is the dividend. My concerns about a potential cut proved to be well-founded, as the quarterly distributions were cut from $0.27 per share to $0.20.

During the Q2 earnings call, management elaborated on the Board's decision to lower it:

We guided in the first quarter that unless we saw a clear path onwards of the near term for the Hercules that we might have just looked at the distribution, call it, longer-term distribution capacity in the company. So it's clear that the rig is warm stacked.

I'll reiterate SFL's business model for a moment. While they are a maritime shipper, they run a diversified business in this industry, owning assets that service multiple types of freight. They write long-term leases on these assets, typically spanning several years.

This portfolio of assets includes two offshore rigs, and the 50% utilization in the slide above reflects the fact that Hercules has no active lease and isn't earning for SFL. It is just creating maintenance costs; note that Q3 revenue for energy assets is $24M against opex of $21M. It's a much tighter margin than the rest of the portfolio because of this. As of Q3, management was not planning to dispose of the asset and was still seeking out a new tenant.

In addition to the dividend cut, total assets have declined by about $250M for the year, driven by dispositions and impairments in the year. Two sales of $57M per vessel were announced in December as well.

Their balance of debt, however, hasn't declined as much, down from $2,839M at the start of the year to $2,760M in Q3, but the cash balance did at least grow from $155M to $278M in that period. The overall trend shows the company trying to boost its liquid position to navigate near-term debt maturities.

Overall, SFL didn't experience serious problems, but the financials reflect a company that feels the weight of its debt.

Outlook and Valuation

At $8.36 per share, SFL offers a 9.57% dividend yield. This is also a slight premium to tangible book value per share, which was $7.47 in Q3. Given the $813M in short-term debt in the Q3 balance sheet, these are both figures that will be stress-tested.

Given the shrinking portfolio, the lack of any announcements for takers on a Hercules lease, and the fact that most of the upcoming debt needs to be repaid or refinanced, I see more room for a dividend cut to manage what is still a tight cash flow situation, as well as reduced TBV per share as assets are trimmed.

The yield is high but may not last long, and a discount to TBV is needed to protect one's principal in case the debt burden leads to some kind of restructuring or liquidation. Again, as their leases are long-term, pretty much the only near-term levers they can pull are dividend cuts or asset sales.

I maintain that SFL is only a Buy if the share price falls below $5 per share (provided such a drop accompanies similar financials) in order to create a basic margin of safety. SFL is not a stock to be purchased lightly.

Looking at the history of TBV per share, it's clear how much their capitalization lends itself to TBV per share erosion. $5 per share gives space for more contraction of the balance sheet as they rotate assets and debt obligations. It also gives a 16% dividend yield, giving something of a risk premium to shareholders.

Q4 2025 results have not been announced yet, but they should be released in about a month. Attention will need to be on the balance sheet and plans for the debt obligations.

Conclusion

Updates in the two quarters that followed were somewhat small, but they were very informative. They confirmed my view that SFL's capitalization makes a yield over 9% less and less likely.

To the extent that such concerns have materialized, SFL is down 21% over the past year. The yield is some form of compensation for this risk, but that's why this isn't a case of undervaluation. I maintain that SFL is a Hold with this assessment.

data.title: Tesla: Robotaxi Storm Clouds To Rain On Parade
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has soared to all-time highs recently due to the excitement over the push towards robots, led by robotaxis. CEO Elon Musk has long promised the robotaxi launch is just around the corner, while a prominent competitor is now full speed ahead on robotaxis. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, with the current valuation pricing in a robotaxi success that isn't clearly visible with the persistent storm clouds likely to rain on the recent parade.

Smoke and Mirrors

Tesla investors have eagerly been awaiting the day when Elon Musk would announce the company has officially launched a robotaxi service. From there, investors and analysts could run the financials similar to ARK Invest and calculate a massive opportunity ahead.

The problem is that Tesla always ends up with a hiccup in the plan for launching unsupervised robotics. Just the fact the market has to distinguish between supervised and unsupervised vehicles is a big part of the problem.

On January 22, investors thought the day had finally come. On Twitter/X, Elon Musk confirmed Tesla was now driving robotaxis around Austin, TX, without a safety monitor. The positive momentum did not last.

Within hours, the market quickly figured out Tesla had moved the safety monitor to a chase vehicle. The EV company has apparently taken the safety monitor out of the robotaxi, but the news made it very unclear if the car is truly driving fully autonomous.

In fact, the Tesla robotaxi account on Twitter/X possibly misleads the market by saving the vehicles we're driving without a safety monitor. At least, Elon Musk stated the monitors were just removed from the car.

Prior to 2025, Elon Musk had made a long list of self-driving vehicle promises going back a decade, with Deepwater Asset Management detailing this long list as follows:

In 2025, Elon Musk ramped up the expectations with the official launch of the robotaxi operation in June following the Cybercab event back in October 2024. The only catch is that Tesla had to launch the business with a safety monitor in Austin and a regular ridesharing business in San Francisco.

The market wasn't worried due to constant promises the official service launch was right around the corner. Elon Musk even proclaimed on the Q2'25 earnings call in July that half the U.S. would have a robotaxi service by the end of 2025.

Musk again scaled back on the Q3'25 earnings call in late October. The CEO claimed Tesla would have robotaxi services in 8 to 10 metro areas by the end of 2025.

Alas, Tesla has started 2026 with more of the same. The company has started the year with more smoke and mirrors and more clear signs the service is far behind the competition.

The difference in the investment from years ago is that Tesla still has the ability to be the leader. The company could launch any day and quickly ramp up robotaxi services due to having manufacturing plants.

The stock is different now because Tesla no longer has a first-mover advantage. Waymo, owned by Alphabet (GOOG), already averages 450,000 robotaxi rides per week, offering services in 6 markets with plans to end 2026 with 1 million rides per week and services in 20 cities.

Every day Tesla is delayed, Waymo becomes more and more the verb for taking a robotaxi, just like googling is a term for an internet search.

The other big issue is that even Waymo is taking upwards of a year from starting testing in a new city to when the company actually launches unsupervised robotaxi services. The company just launched in Miami after announcing plans in late 2024 to start testing in early 2025.

Tesla isn't currently testing services in any other city and would need to provide regulators with testing data before an approval likely occurs. The quick ramps predicted by Elon Musk don't appear possible when even the robotaxi leader isn't accomplishing such moves.

Weak Results Require Robotaxi Success

Even if investors didn't always believe those promises, the market has bought into the most recent ones. For Q3, Tesla reported sales only rose 11% due to weak EV demand, and profits fell by 31% to an EPS of only $0.50.

The business is struggling, and new EV competition is heating up with Rivian Automotive (RIVN) launching the price-competitive R2 this year and Lucid Group (LCID) still in the game with increased production and the launch of a midsize crossover in 2026. Globally, the Chinese EV companies like BYD (BYDDF)(BYDDY) remain huge threats to Tesla, so the company isn't guaranteed to grow EV sales outside of a successful robotaxi.

Tesla will report Q4'25 results on January 28. The market isn't expecting anything better for the December quarter, with Q4 deliveries already reported to have fallen by 16% to 418K due in large part to the loss of tax credits.

  • EPS of $0.45, down 38% YoY
  • Revenue of $24.75 billion, down 3.7% YoY

The ARK Invest robotaxi opportunity probably has some realistic aspects to the market overall. The issue is that Tesla is no longer a first mover likely to capture the majority of the market, with Waymo already entrenched with 20 million rides completed and the likes of Zoox, owned by Amazon (AMZN), offering services in Las Vegas. On a global basis, Tesla already faces at least 5 robotaxi providers offering services already.

The market will be far more split up, though potentially enormous. ARK Invest outlined the potential need for 600k robotaxis in Austin alone, with larger cities reaching the millions, creating a $34 trillion enterprise value for the AV technology providers using a $1.8 trillion EBIT number and a 19x multiple.

The numbers are enormous, but Tesla has to actually launch services. For now, the stock trades at enormous valuations, such as over 206x forward EPS targets.

Tesla is currently worth $1.5 trillion. The stock could have some upside potential on any signs the company has finally unlocked the robotaxi opportunity and will scale faster than the competition. Otherwise, the valuation is just unsustainable based on recent results.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Tesla shows no signs of actually executing on the robotaxi opportunity. The stock shouldn't trade based on this premium, with Tesla and Elon Musk great at building new untapped markets where creativity and drive are more important than execution.

Investors should continue to use the current elevated stock price to sell Tesla on the ongoing robotaxi farce.

data.title: Newmont Is A Golden Opportunity The Market Is Undervaluing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am initiating a Strong Buy rating on Newmont Corporation (NYSE:NEM) for a couple of reasons. First of all, I believe that NEM provides a golden opportunity given the current valuation numbers. Secondly, the company has been delivering record cash flows, and this is evident from the Q3 earnings report. Also, the company just launched production at a new gold mine, the Ahafo mine in Ghana. It is also important to mention that the company has been undergoing some sort of restructuring, or rather what I would call reorganization, in order to streamline its operations. Yet despite all these positives, the company is priced as if they don't exist.

Just to give some context on where the company stood by Q3 2025, NEM ended Q3 with $0 debt after paying down its last payment of $2Bn. So ideally, as of now, the company is debt-free. Also, it ended the quarter after generating a record $4.5Bn in FCFs. Also, Moody's upgraded NEM's issuer credit rating to A3 from Baa1, with a stable outlook. So clearly, I would say there are many tailwinds around the Newmont. Yet these do not reflect on the current price. For instance, on a growth-adjusted basis, I note that the company is trading at 0.32x, which is a 77% discount to the sector median. I will discuss more on valuation in the valuation section.

Business Overview

So, what does Newmont Corporation do? Well, NEM is among the largest gold producers in the world. It has over 12 sites across different regions such as North America, Australia, South America, and Africa as well. One of the key aspects that I would like to highlight about NEM is the fact that it has been able to navigate some of the largest mergers in the mining industry. It also has some small exposure to copper.

Growth Drivers

So, what makes me believe that Newmont will push higher in the coming quarters?

There are a couple of growth drivers that I believe will push NEM to greater heights. First of all, as I mentioned earlier, the company underwent a reorganization of their management and decentralized their decision-making process. So, as a result, the company was able to consolidate four regions into two business units. And this positions NEM in a better place, as noted by the CEO, Tom Palmer, "...sharpens accountability and simplifies how we work, empowering local teams and enabling faster and more agile execution..."

At the start of 2025, NEM underwent a reorganization whereby they reduced the number of senior management teams and decentralized decision-making. They also consolidated from four regions to two business units. As CEO Tom Palmer put it, the structure "...sharpens accountability and simplifies how we work, empowering local teams and enabling faster and more agile execution...". Now, in my opinion, it means that NEM has been able to cut down on bureaucracy. And this makes it more cost efficient. Of course, this should translate into better execution and higher margins moving forward. And already, the restructuring and reorganization are already delivering results. By eliminating layers of management and driving down labor and contractor costs, NEM was able to improve its 2025 cost guidance. And remember, this is while maintaining production targets. So moving forward, I expect the cost discipline to continue bearing fruit in 2026 and beyond.

As I mentioned earlier, Newmont is not just cutting costs. But it is also boosting output with new projects. And like I said, the most immediate catalyst is the Ahafo North Mine in Ghana. It is a brand-new, high-grade gold operation that just achieved commercial production in Q4 2025, as noted by the CEO;

...our new exciting mine... that adds profitable gold production over an initial 13 years of mine life...

This means that Ahafo will ramp up to produce on the order of 300,000 ounces per year, offsetting declines at the adjacent Ahafo South mine and expanding NEM's footprint in West Africa. I would say this is growth that one can touch. And this is because ounces are coming online now, and not hopes that are pinned on a distant discovery. With the estimated first gold pour already in September and full commercial status reached in October, Ahafo North's cash flow impact will start accruing immediately in Q4 and scale up in 2026.

... We will also declare commercial production by the end of today at our new existing mine. Ahafo North, which expands our existing footprint in Ghana and adds profitable gold production over an initial 13 years of mine life...

At the end of the day, Newmont's production base is literally expanding. Ahafo North will fuel volume growth and earnings, acting as a fresh engine of cash generation for the next decade.

Looking further out, NEM's project pipeline remains full of low-hanging fruit. In Australia, the Tanami Expansion 2 is nearing completion. It is a 1.5 km deep production shaft to increase output and cut costs at the Tanami underground mine. Again, when I look at Papua New Guinea, the massive Lihir operation is optimizing its mine plan to access higher-grade ore. And in NE's newly added Canadian portfolio, two new block cave mines at the giant Cadia copper-gold complex are advancing development. These projects underscore the growth runway ahead. NEM's management is exercising capital discipline. It is important to mention that there are few gold miners that have such a rich pipeline of Tier 1 assets to develop. And this is why Newmont's pipeline is the envy of the industry and gives me confidence that the company can keep growing production into the late 2020s without chasing risky acquisitions.

Now, let's move into how the market is pricing Newmont today

Valuation

When I look at the valuation numbers, there are a couple of things that catch my attention. First and foremost, the stock's valuation remains very undemanding. On an FWD-looking basis, the shares trade around 18x FWD Non-GAAP P/E. This is in line with the sector median, which stands at 18.86x. Secondly, on an EV/EBITDA basis, the company is trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA of 9.86x while the sector median stands at 9.96x, which I would say is in line with the sector median.

From a growth-adjusted perspective, I note that the company has an FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio of 0.32x. Yet the sector median stands at 1.40x. And this implies that NEM is trading at a 77% discount to the sector median, as shown above. And this is because its FWD revenue growth stands at 28%, while the median is at 3%. Ideally, it means that currently, investors are paying 32 cents for each $1 of Newmont's growth, whereas the average gold stock goes for $1.4 per growth dollar. In my view, this valuation disconnect is why I am rating Newmont a strong buy.

When I compare some of the peers as shown below, clearly I am convinced that NEM is trading below most of its major peers.

FWD Non-GAAP P/E FWD EV/EBITDA FWD Non-GAAP PEG
Newmont Corp (NEM) 18.68 9.86 0.32
Agnico Eagle (AEM) 25.15 12.63 0.7
Barrick (B) 21.5 9.31 0.47
AngloGold (AU) 18.46 8.73 NA

Also, the share buybacks are a great gauge of how the stock is undervalued. And as such, it is a great vote of confidence, especially given COO Natascha Vilijoen's sentiments on the same.

...with this wealth of the portfolio that we have, the best investment for us is in our own assets and in share buybacks... we will remain disciplined around that...

Newmont's Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for Newmont is gold price volatility. As with any gold miner, NEM's fortunes are largely tied to the fall and rise of the price of gold. If gold were to plunge significantly, NEM's revenues and margins would shrink accordingly. This also implies that a sustained gold downturn would undoubtedly pressure the stock and is the key macro risk to my thesis. In my article titled "Fourmile Discovery Is A One-In-A-Lifetime Opportunity For Barrick Mining," I talked about Barrick, which also happens to be a key competitor of Newmont Corporation. In that piece, I talked about political instability as a key risk. Similarly, NEM is also exposed to political risk. And this is due to the fact that it also has some of its major gold mines in the Western Africa region. Of late, the region has been faced with coups and military interruptions. This means that if some of the countries where NEM has mines are affected, then it means that this could affect NEM's operations.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, Newmont is a globally diversified behemoth that has proven it can grow and innovate even in a mature industry. NEM transformed itself by acquiring top-tier assets, pouring first gold at a new mine, and tightening up its cost structure. My conclusion is that Newmont has a unique combination of scale, pipeline, and execution that will create substantial value in the coming years. And that's why I am giving the stock a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: San Juan Basin Royalty Trust: Time To End This Impractical Idea
data.description:
There is nothing like an income vehicle that pays no income when the product (in this case natural gas) has a period of relatively strong prices. Of course, there is a legitimate reason for that. But there is also no reason for investors to speculate as to when the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust (SJT) might resume royalty payments or even if it might resume royalty payments. There are plenty of good solid vehicles out there for either appreciation potential or income plays that likely can do a less complicated job as well as a likely better job for any investor than is the case here.

It is not that there is anything necessarily wrong. Instead, it is far more likely that the best acreage has, for at least the time being, been produced and now the acreage being developed is less profitable. It is also possible that the period of weak natural gas prices that is ending has been taking its toll on a play like this. But whatever the reason, there has been more than enough time for distributions to resume. As the latest report shows, it will still likely be a while before there are any distributions to investors.

Since production declines each month, the longer this situation persists with no distributions, the lower production will be if and when distributions resume. So, the income picture in the future declines with each month that there are still more repayments due.

This has been a friendly winter so far with a fair number of arctic blasts. There may even be a few more before it is over with. But seasonally weak natural gas prices come next after winter. It then depends upon a very hot summer to raise natural gas prices again so that a serious "dent" can be put into the excess costs and the credit line which needs to be repaid first. This is quickly becoming an untenable situation. I personally do not need to stay until the end.

Latest Finances

The latest monthly statement showed a balance of $5,633,310 plus (as the last article mentioned) there is a $2,000,000 line of credit to supply cash until those excess costs are paid. The going concern statement is also an issue from the last article.

The last thing that investors need is favorable weather to determine whether or not there will be anymore payments. The thing to remember also is that production declines each month as well. So, the longer this whole situation "goes on", the less likely it is that investors will receive any significant distributions before the next drilling campaign.

If anything, it is far more likely that the "going concern" warning mentioned in the last article happens. To me, the risk of a material investment principal loss is much too high for investors to consider this anymore.

I follow far too many capital appreciation vehicles and income vehicles that are much safer that this for the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust to be considered for investors. It is honestly at the point where it is hard to see a positive outcome for investors looking into the long-term. This is definitely not for income investors anymore.

The latest press release mentioned that the total owed went down roughly $700,000 in very round numbers in the latest month. But that was a good month. The balances shown above mean that there needs to be 10 of these good months in a row before a distribution is even considered. Frankly at this point this is a rather impractical idea than an investment proposal. I throw in the towel at this point.

Alternative Appreciation Idea

For those considering this idea for appreciation with the idea that the stock price will go up when there is finally a distribution, I would instead direct them to Comstock Resources (CRK). My last article on this company reviews the cash raising strategy that has so far been successful without diluting shareholders (and definitely without a going concern statement).

Comstock Resources has a brand-new Western Haynesville discovery that is looking pretty good in the early stages. Management still has work to do to derisk the acreage and get costs down from where they are now.

But as far as appreciation potential goes, the outlook there is far better than is the case here with that new discovery.

Alternative Income Idea

Viper Energy (VNOM) is down considerably from its high point due to an acquisition that is supposed to turn out to be accretive. Whereas the San Juan Royalty Trust really only has natural gas exposure, Viper Energy has natural gas exposure with a significant oil exposure that may also be pressuring the price of the stock.

However, unlike the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust, Viper Energy is paying a distribution that has from time to time crept (barely) into high yield territory. The recovery potential of that distribution is substantial should natural gas prices strengthen as predicted and oil prices recover. But at least now, during a period of weak prices, there is a strong balance sheet with no going concern statement, and there is a distribution being paid.

Summary

Either or both of the two alternatives that I have suggested (Comstock Resources and Viper Energy) are likely to prove superior in returns to the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust

Frankly the promise of a renewal of trust distributions is looking more and more like an impossible feat. Instead, it would appear that all the costs will be repaid in time for new wells to be drilled that will begin the process all over again.

You also have a going concern statement (as the last article noted). That makes this idea really inappropriate for income investors and really restricts this idea to investors with a lot of training in a situation like this. This is a situation where you have strict criteria to make money and then you walk away from it not caring what the stock price does after you are done. Such an investor is a fairly high-level investor (definitely not the average investor).

Therefore, this idea remains a very strong sell because there are likely other ideas with as much or more positive return potential but without the worries of long-term principal loss that exist here.

Also, some of my ideas like Viper Energy are a corporation which simplifies the tax situation that exists here tremendously.

Risks

This situation is rapidly becoming an investment idea with no practical way to a positive return. Frankly, the trust needs too long a period of strong commodity prices to repay all the money it owes for investors to consider this idea. The weather is generally not that helpful.

Even if the company were to pay down all the debt owed and begin distributions, it would very likely be time to drill on the acreage again and start the repayment process all over.

The last risk is the going concern statement mentioned in the last article that I really do not see going away any time soon. If anything, I suspect that the chances of that warning actually happening, meaning the trust goes broke, as a significant risk.

There has got to be a better way for any investor to invest than is the case here. Personally, I think the risk at current prices is even too great for traders.

data.title: Microsoft: Cloud Adoption Remains As The Top Growth Catalyst (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) may be faced with increased investor scrutiny, as enterprise AI adoption remains relatively sluggish despite the billions of dollars of investments made in application development and compute capacity. With the greatest paradigm shift in physical AI adoption still years away, investors must weigh whether short-term headwinds will cloud the long-term growth story. Despite near-term AI adoption challenges, Microsoft remains well-positioned for growth for its cloud computing services, with or without the use of AI. I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for MSFT shares with a price target of $646/share at 20.71x eFY27 EV/EBITDA.

Microsoft Q2 ’26 Earnings Expectations

Microsoft is set to report Q2 ’26 earnings on January 28, 2026, after market close. Despite multiple quarters of strong growth in cloud computing, shares have experienced significant chop throughout 2025 and have slid since November 2025 as the AI theme grows stale with investors. With more investors expecting an AI bubble burst, investing in the mega-cap technology companies may hold substantially greater risk given their market capitalization dominance and share of the cloud computing market.

Despite the relatively harsh outlook for AI, I don’t believe the hyperscalers will necessarily be substantially impacted by the broader AI bubble theme in the sense of operational hardship. I believe a critical factor that is oftentimes overlooked is that Microsoft’s cloud business isn’t dependent on AI development but rather on cloud hosting services. I believe AI applications are largely an additive rather than the entirety of the cloud service offerings. Investors must bear in mind that Microsoft’s remaining performance obligations sit at $398b.

On top of this, I believe it is easy to overlook that AI development is still in the early stages of existence, with OpenAI’s (OPENAI) ChatGPT only hitting the market in late 2022. Though the novelty of the chatbot has worn off, AI growth and development are far from over. Accordingly, agentic AI is still narrowly adopted across enterprises given the novelty of the applications. I have no expectations of adoption occurring overnight, given how critical it is for enterprises to operate without a hitch, which may mean that agentic AI will be a slow burn over the coming years rather than a one-time growth acceleration.

Nonetheless, slow adoption has been noted at Microsoft, particularly for its Copilot application. I believe the biggest challenge faced isn’t necessarily the development of Copilot but more so the functionality and use cases for the AI assistant. Chatbots can only provide as much value as their use. Despite the slow adoption, I don’t believe the AI story ends with Copilot.

At its analyst day in late 2025, GlobalFoundries (GFS) outlined a multi-year roadmap for the development of physical AI as part of its growth strategy, telling me that the largest industrial impact from AI development has yet to take place. Smart factories, a market Microsoft is addressing, may be the big paradigm shift for AI as information technology and operational technology converge with physical AI as the centerpiece. Though I believe there will be localized data storage for inferencing, I’m expecting a substantial amount of data flow will occur through a cloud-based environment to ensure functionality and effective operations.

The challenge posed is that broad adoption of physical AI isn’t expected to really ramp up until later in the decade, presenting investors with a 2-4-year gap between now and then. Despite the long-term catalyst, I believe near-term adoption of Copilot or other enterprise AI applications may add some uncertainty for investors and may potentially cloud the outlook for more advanced applications. I believe the biggest risk for investors is to use today’s AI tools as the benchmark for development, whereas advancement in physical AI may be the ultimate goal.

This could ultimately impact investor sentiment towards Microsoft’s Q2 ’26 earnings, particularly if AI adoption is the centerpiece of conversation. As for the risk of an AI bubble, MSFT shares are trading at a modest premium at 19.46x EV/EBITDA, a slight premium to the peer average of 18.76x.

The bottom line is that I don’t believe the hyperscalers will be significantly impacted by the perceived AI bubble. The biggest factor is that each of the 4 major hyperscalers, Oracle Corp. (ORCL) excluded, hold a substantial amount of cash on the balance sheet and generate a substantial amount of free cash flow despite having spent billions of dollars on expanding compute capacity in the last few years.

Microsoft Financial Forecast

Looking to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting Microsoft to generate $80.75b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $3.99/share. I’m expecting the Intelligent Cloud segment to remain strong in the quarter, growing at nearly 28% when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Despite the strong growth outlook, I’m expecting an incremental margin contraction for the segment to be just shy of 42% in Q2 ’26.

Risks Related to Microsoft

Bull Case

Microsoft remains in a position of strength with a substantial RPO for cloud compute capacity, supporting the firm’s data center investment cycle. Despite the headwinds in Copilot adoption, I do not believe that this challenge will significantly influence cash flow for the firm.

Bear Case

AI adoption is growing at a slower pace than initially expected a few years back, potentially leading to increased investor scrutiny regarding the investment theme. These near-term hurdles could cloud the long-term outlook and result in investors losing confidence in the AI theme, sending MSFT shares lower.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

MSFT shares are currently trading at 19.46x EV/EBITDA, a relative premium when compared to peer hyperscalers. Given the increased investor scrutiny towards the AI theme, I believe it would be prudent to expect a more moderate trading premium on future performance. Despite this adjustment, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for MSFT shares with a price target of $646/share at 20.71x eFY27 EV/EBITDA.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: VSE Corporation: M&A As An Aerospace Growth Engine
data.description:
I have been covering a wide range of aerospace and defense companies. I believe that the benefit that aerospace and defense companies offer is long-term stable growth trajectory with higher growth in commercial aerospace and modest growth in defense. Currently we see that both end markets are experiencing accelerating growth and that makes stocks in the aerospace and defense industries extremely attractive and also is why I am continuously looking to expand my coverage. In this report, I will be adding VSE Corporation (VSEC) to my coverage.

VSE Corporation Sharpens Focus On Commercial Aerospace Growth

VSE Corporation started out as a services and parts provider for transportation assets with a focus on aviation. In the past few years, the company has shifted it focus completely to aviation.

Initially, the company had three segments:

  1. Federal & Defense Services.
  2. Wheeler Fleet Solutions.
  3. Aviation.

Perhaps somewhat surprisingly, VSE sold the Federal & Defense Services segment. In 2023, the company announced that the segment would be sold in a $100 million deal. However, that deal was terminated in the same year as the transaction was not expected to be completed in the near term. In 2024, the segment was ultimately sold for $42.9 million. Given the growing demand for defense equipment and services, this may be seen as a surprising move. However, we note that commercial aerospace still has the highest longer-term growth trends and more than likely the defense business that VSE had was lower growth and lower margin.

At some point, especially if there is a risk of substantial decline in revenues due to sunsetting programs within the segment, divesture becomes a logical step. In April 2025, the company also sold the Fleet segment for $136.2 million (10Q, Page 14). This step completes the refocus on aviation aftermarket sales through divestures.

After the divesture of the Defense business, the company acquired Turbine Controls which focuses on MRO support services for engine components for commercial and military applications. So, we do note that the company did not fully move away from the defense market. Defense sales, however, are now part of the company’s focus on aviation aftermarket solutions. In the same year, Desser Aerospace was acquired.

In 2025, the company also acquired Turbine Weld specializing in MRO services for complex technical and proprietary engine components for business jets and general aviation. In October 2025, another acquisition followed in which the company announced that it would acquire Aero 3 for $350 million.

So, we see that the company started to refocus on aviation in 2023-2024 and from there initiated a path to strengthen the MRO footprint and portfolio. In that regard, VSE Corporation is following a path that we see among many providers of MRO. As demand for maintenance, repair and overhaul is increasing more companies are engaged in acquisitions to grow the footprint. VSE Corporation is now engaged in parts distribution, MRO and supply chain & inventory services.

The Opportunities And Risks For VSE Corporation

The obvious growth opportunity for the company is the growth in MRO and related services. MRO itself is expected to grow by 2.7% annually through 2035 according to Oliver Wyman. That is actually not a growth rate that impresses me. Within the MRO market, airframe growth is expected to be the slowest grower at 1.3% followed by 2.5% for engines and above-average growth for line and components. One reason why the growth may not look attractive is for the simple reason that demand for MRO has grown substantially after the pandemic as the lack of new airplane deliveries increased aftermarket demand to keep airplanes in service longer.

So, the growth in MRO demand in the years ahead is partially looking somewhat soft because the base year for the growth calculation is also higher than what it would normally have been. The relatively low growth in MRO is also one of the reasons why companies are increasingly looking to grow inorganically.

The market for parts distribution is expected to grow 8% annually through 2032, while MRO logistics growth is expected to grow around 7% annually through 2034. Those are nicer growth figures. So, just MRO is not the real growth driver. It is the combination with parts and distribution that hosts a lot of values for companies and investors.

Next to opportunities, there are also risks. No opportunity comes without risk. The dominant risks for VSE are cyclicality and parts availability. We also note that macroeconomic pressures in combination with easing in MRO demand through new airplane deliveries could results in overall softness. For companies with an acquisition focused approach, there also is the risk of integration. Acquisitions are not solely done to increase the footprint and portfolio, but also to realize cost savings and those savings may not always materialize.

Furthermore, we note that with MRO companies currently being engaged in a round of M&A, competitive pressures in the market are rising and finding good deals for M&A may become increasingly more difficult. We also note that M&A comes at a cost, either through increasing debt or shareholder dilution. As an example, the acquisition of Aero 3 is funded through a public stock offering . Overall, I am marking the risk for VSE Corporation moderate.

VSE Corporation Is A Higher Risk Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For VSE Corporation, the business case actually sounds nicer than the investment case. I do have a Buy rating on VSE Corporation, stock but we do note that the company has a history of diluting shareholders to finance acquisitions. So, that is definitely a risk. However, at the same time I also do believe that the business scaling will help the company improve the financial performance.

I am currently expecting 11% annual growth on sales, 23% annual growth in EBITDA and free cash flow to inflect to positive territory. Without dilution risk, the price target would have been $249.77 implying 21% upside. However, I am taking a more conservative approach implementing only half of the envisioned upside bringing the price target to $227.89. We do note that the company is trading substantially ahead of its median EV/EBITDA, but I do believe that is plausible given that the company has now fully pivoted to aerospace and defense and that means that historic EV/EBITDA values are not the best benchmark for valuing the stock anymore.

We also see that the market is pricing the stock in line with 2026 earnings on a peer group EV/EBITDA, and I believe that does make sense. It means that there is little upside for the stock driven by earnings for this year, but adopting a forward looking view there is substantial upside.

We see that besides sales growth, the company also expects margins to improve from 12.6% to 17.2% by 2027. More importantly, as the company scales up the free cash flow should inflect to positive territory in 2026. That will also give the company a better capital intensity against operating cash flow. The risk with VSE Corporation has been that it dilutes shareholders to finance acquisitions. We cannot rule out that this will happen again in the future, but we do note that with expectations of positive free cash flow the company should be able to finance more acquisitions through cash and perhaps more realistically with net debt leverage falling into a more appreciable range we should also see better access to debt financing.

The company is targeting a net debt leverage of 3x-3.5x over the longer-term. That suggests that future acquisitions will likely be financed through debt, thereby reducing the risk of shareholder dilution.

Conclusion: VSE Corporation Is A Buy With Some Risks

The fact that VSE Corporation has pivoted to aviation is no major surprise. What perhaps is more surprising is that the company was not generating positive free cash flows. Positive free cash flows are currently expected after the acquisition of new businesses and that also means that in my view the “existing business” is not as strong in terms of cash flow as I would have liked. Divesture of the Fleet business earlier in 2025 may have helped somewhat in the cash flow performance, but I wouldn’t say that the company had the scale for sustained free cash flow growth.

The cash flow growth we see in the years ahead primarily comes from the combination of organic growth with a thick layer of inorganic growth. That doesn’t necessarily mean that this is a bad business to be invested in, but also means that we will more than likely see continued M&A to fuel growth and rising net debt leverages as a result.

data.title: JELD-WEN Holding: More Pain Is On The Horizon (Downgrade)
data.description:
The last few months have been a very painful time for shareholders of JELD-WEN Holding (JELD). Back in late October of last year, I called the company a "Hold" candidate. At the time, financial performance had been rapidly deteriorating. Revenue, profits, and cash flows were all dropping. This was driven by a weak housing market and weak spending when it came to remodeling. Of course, some of the pain also came from the firm's decision to sell off its Towanda enterprise, though at least we could argue that was a one-time thing. Tariffs are certainly proving to be problematic and could pose a risk for 2026. And since that article was published, shares have plummeted, falling 27.4%, while the S&P 500 is up 1.5%.

Naturally, I am not very ecstatic about this. And unfortunately, I think that worse things could come. I know that many investors are optimistic because of declining interest rates. At some point, I suspect that those will ultimately boost housing demand and remodeling activity. But I think we are heading toward a recession before that point occurs. And if we do hit such a point, especially in light of tariff-related pain that management has already forecasted, further downside is probable. That's why I am making the unfortunate decision to downgrade the stock to a "Sell" right now.

Significant Pain

I doubt that there is any way to describe what is going on with JELD-WEN Holding other than to call the pain significant. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $809.5 million. That was down from the $934.7 million the company reported a year earlier. This drop, as painful as it was, was driven in part by the court-ordered divestiture of the company’s Towanda business. For context, that part of the company was focused on producing interior molded door skins that were used in the construction of finished interior doors. However, when management completed the acquisition of the business back in 2012, it gave them a significant amount of control over a very niche market in the United States. On antitrust concerns, the firm was ultimately forced to sell off the enterprise in exchange for $115 million.

This divestiture hit revenue to the tune of about 5% year over year. However, core revenues for the company dropped a solid 10%. This was even though management increased prices by 1%, with the hit being driven by an 11% plunge in volume and product mix. Unfortunately, industry conditions are challenging right now. Years of inflationary pressures and high-interest rates aimed at combating them have made housing unaffordable for many. That's why, for 2025 in its entirety, management expected North American volumes to drop at a high single-digit rate, with new single-family construction down at a high single-digit rate, repair and remodeling activity down similarly, and multifamily and Canadian sales down 10% or greater. North America isn't the only problem area for the company. In Europe, they are forecasting a mid-single-digit volume decline, with residential construction down at a mid-single-digit rate and a slight drop in commercial projects.

The decline in revenue for the company brought with it lower profits as well. The firm went from a loss of $73 million to a loss of $367.6 million. Most of that hit came from impairment charges worsening from $63.4 million to $196.9 million. However, there were non-cash items in this as well. Operating cash flow ended up dropping as a result, plunging from $37.6 million to $11.2 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $26.5 million to $15.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business dropped precipitously from $91 million to $53.7 million. It is also worth noting that adjusted net profits declined from $27.6 million to negative $17.3 million. So even if we exclude one-time events, the picture here was painful.

The third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was not a blip on the radar for the company. In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time a year earlier. Overwhelmingly, the company was negatively affected by lower volume and changes in product mix. Core revenue dropped 13% year over year because of this. And as a result, profitability was also slammed. In fact, management recently decided to cut their workforce by 11% to cut down on costs. And one thing I worry about is that this might not be enough. The good news is that management is looking at other opportunities as well, including potentially making some strategic changes when it comes to its operations in Europe. The firm is also working to cut costs by simplifying its product portfolio, which should ultimately reduce complexity.

You see, according to the company, the total tariff impact last year should be about $17 million. But that's nothing. On an annualized basis, the company expects a tariff impact of $45 million. This means that, without factoring in mitigation strategies, 2026 could be worse than 2025. When it comes to 2025, management said that revenue would be somewhere between $3.1 billion and $3.2 billion. That compares to the $3.78 billion reported for 2024. We should also expect a decline in EBITDA from $275.2 million to between $105 million and $120 million. And if we project out results based on what we saw for the first nine months of 2025, adjusted operating cash flow would come in at around $27.4 million.

These estimates make it easy for us to value the company. In the chart above, you can see exactly what I mean. The company is actually not expensive, even with the recent pain that we have experienced. In fact, it's teetering between being fairly valued and undervalued. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is not expensive either, as the same chart above illustrates. Two of the companies are cheaper than they are on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, though four are cheaper when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. But again, this does not factor in the prospect of additional weakening that I believe will occur because of broader economic weakness. This is something that I have covered in other articles, but my general assessment is that tariffs and persistently high-interest rates will push consumers to even more extreme circumstances than where they have been recently.

At some point, the picture will improve. As the chart below illustrates, home prices have already been dropping in the United States. That should make housing more affordable. But unfortunately, this also puts downward pressure on the company since it will also be pressured by end users to lower its prices. If it doesn't, additional volume declines are probable. The only thing that might prove beneficial is once interest rates fall enough and economic conditions stabilize to the point where homebuilding activity accelerates.

Currently, that's not happening. The most recent data covers through October 2025. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, housing completions that month were 15.3% lower than they were the previous year. Housing starts were down 7.8%, while building permits were down 1.1%. The good news is that there is a big catalyst here that should prove beneficial for market participants at some point. And this relates to the fact that there exists a large and persistent housing shortage in the United States. Estimates vary, but one source pegged the shortage at 2.8 million units, with the expectation that this could take around 10 years to resolve. Another source put the figure higher at 4.7 million homes. But regardless, this is meaningful, and once interest rates drop enough and economic conditions stabilize, home building activity is likely to increase. If JELD-WEN Holding can stay afloat long enough for this to materialize, there could be some nice upside. But timing here is speculative.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that while JELD-WEN Holding is interesting, it's not an ideal candidate right now. In fact, because I expect economic conditions to deteriorate more rapidly than I expected previously, I would argue that a bearish assessment, even a modest one, would be appropriate. That's why I've decided to downgrade the stock to a soft "Sell."

data.title: IMCG: Solid Vehicle, But Not The Time To Buy
data.description:
The iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG), launched on 06/28/2004 by BlackRock, Inc. and managed by BlackRock Fund Advisors, provides exposure to stocks with growth characteristics within the mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity market. It manages ~$3.2 billion and charges an expense ratio of 0.06%.

The fund is the cheapest mid-cap growth ETF and has performed well against peers over time. The approach to assessing growth is reasonable, too. However, the current environment and valuation point to potential underperformance relative to the broad market and large-cap growth portfolios.

Methodology

The ETF uses the Morningstar US Mid Cap Broad Growth Index as a benchmark. First, you should understand that the parent index (Morningstar US Mid Cap Index) has a very specific way of splitting the size segments, and it's this:

  1. Large-cap: stocks that make up the top 70% of the U.S. equity market
  2. Mid-cap: stocks that fall between 70% and 90% of the market
  3. Small-cap: stocks that belong in the bottom 90%–99.5% of the market

The benchmark index assesses a growth score for each constituent of the parent. The score is calculated by taking into account the values of the forecast growth rates per share for EPS, cash flow, revenue, and book value. If it's available, the index will also consider the 5-year forecast of long-term earnings growth sourced by a third party, the value of which will contribute to the score by 50%, with the rest of the values sharing the other half.

This is a purely fundamental approach with a significant forward-looking anchor, checking the box of the most sensible way to define growth stocks and avoiding the "sins" of funds taking a shortcut. Some other indices will also use valuation metrics, assuming that a high valuation implies high-growth expectations; though this is correct for the most part, it risks pulling in names that are highly valued because of sentiment rather than growth potential. On top of this, there are indices that use momentum as a proxy, too; the inclusion of which makes past price behavior a contributor to the definition of growth, blurring the distinction between growth and momentum. Then there's the common approach of defining growth based on historical data alone, which is a poor way to assess growth potential because it baselessly assumes that what's been happening should persist.

A drawback to Morningstar's approach is that forecast growth rates are prone to revision, and the per-share growth can be distorted by buybacks. It's a case of choosing the lesser evil instead of making the methodology even more complicated.

I also appreciate that when it comes to weighting, there's no binary in/out decision being made. The index is market-cap weighted, and some stocks that belong to the blend category will be included in the index, but only a fraction of their market cap will be taken into account for weighting them, according to how close and far they are to growth and value, respectively.

Reconstitution occurs semi-annually, on the third Friday of June and December, while rebalancing is done on a quarterly basis, on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December.

Portfolio

The weights of the top names indicate a low concentration risk, as is usual for mid-cap and small-cap ETFs; no single stock accounts for more than 1.33% of the portfolio, and the top 10 account for only 11.14%. Consistent with Morningstar's mid-cap segment and the growth bias, the top holdings point to a blended exposure to cyclical sectors and tech. The catch is that the tech stocks are related to hardware (Marvell (MRVL), Seagate (STX), Corning (GLW)), which doesn't represent a differentiated driver to the same degree that software does.

With the core being Industrials and Technology, the above indication is further supported; this is a blend of cyclical drivers and AI infrastructure. Consumer Cyclical and Financials are also meaningful contributors, which would benefit the fund in a soft-landing scenario and improve credit conditions. Healthcare, Utilities, and Consumer Defensive stocks have more modest weights, which is also consistent with the growth bias here.

Alternatives

When it comes to mid-cap growth, there is actually no cheaper fund than IMCG, based on its 0.06% expense ratio. The second cheapest option is the Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth (VOT), which has a much higher AUM size at $18 billion. The core difference is that it calculates growth differently; it uses future long-term and short-term earnings growth, as well as 3-year earnings and revenue growth. It also uses the investment/assets ratio and ROA as proxies for potential growth. For what it's worth, I find the expense ratio differential is so negligible here that Vanguard's more sophisticated score methodology makes it a theoretically superior option. Plus, it reconstitutes and rebalances quarterly.

For a blend of growth and momentum, investors will need to pay more in annual fees. The iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth (IJK) takes into account 12-month price momentum on top of the 3-year EPS change to price ratio and 3-year revenue per share growth. Its expense ratio is 0.18%.

Performance

When it comes to its long-term performance, IMCG has outperformed its peers by a meaningful margin. However, it's worth noting that IJK, with its more simplistic methodology and higher fees, was consistently outperforming for the largest part of the previous decade. Plus, the return differential has been produced recently and not from consistent excess returns year after year.

IJK has also been less volatile in the last ten years, with a slightly lower annualized standard deviation and a meaningfully less severe maximum drawdown. IMCG has performed better in absolute and risk-adjusted returns, though. Still, no fund outperformed the S&P 500.

Regardless, I view 10-year performance metrics as noisy because the regimes they include make them so idiosyncratic and not reflective of how effective the approach is. I think that the 5-year chart below is a good reflection of the great overlap these funds have with each other in terms of exposure and drivers because they performed quite similarly in both bear and bull markets.

Risks

Although growth stocks are likely to outperform in a soft-landing scenario, which seems likely now, mid-cap growth stocks are unlikely to outperform large-cap growth. As we saw above, the tech exposure is significant, but the top holdings point to something that is consistent with non-large-cap tech stocks: software businesses, especially those related to AI, are not as dominant in the segment. There is an opportunity risk present here because large-cap growth portfolios have more direct exposure to non-cyclical drivers such as AI and cloud adoption, and slowing economic growth should impact mid-cap growth more.

Another risk that is perhaps longer-term is the valuation of mid-cap growth stocks. IMCG's P/E and P/B ratios, as of January 23, are 34.2x and 6x, respectively. For reference, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) has an earnings multiple of 30x and a Price-to-Book ratio of 5.5x.

Conclusion

I don't believe that mid-cap growth exposure is a good idea right now. As a long-term vehicle, IMCG will do exactly what it says on the tin, and very cheaply at that. But with the current risks, I find the mid-cap growth bias unlikely to bring any value to investors. I am rating IMCG a hold.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: Global Net Lease: No Further Dividend Cuts Yet, But Shares Are A Sell
data.description:
I was previously a shareholder of Global Net Lease (NYSE:GNL) in the late 2010s. However, I sold my GNL stock in August 2019 after the company reduced its dividend. While there are some cases where a REIT remains a good investment after a dividend cut, frequently it's a prudent strategy to exit the position once a cut occurs.

Because, as it would turn out, oftentimes, a REIT that cuts its dividend once ends up cutting again and again in the years to come. Global Net Lease has been one such example, as this chart demonstrates:

The REIT, which formerly paid more than $2/share in dividends annually, has reduced its dividend on various occasions. The dividend has now slipped to just $0.93 per share per year, a more than 50% cumulative reduction.

There can be some grace for dividend cuts that occurred during the pandemic, as that event caused unprecedented stress for many real estate tenants. However, it's important to note that the first big dividend cut came back in 2019, prior to the onset of the pandemic. And then, in more recent years, Global Net Lease has further reduced its dividend in late 2023, 2024, and once again early last year.

From the continuing pattern of dividend cuts in recent years, it seems that Global Net Lease's issues aren't just a one-time blip tied to the pandemic or near-term economic jitters but rather a sustained downturn in the REIT's operational trajectory.

And that has now been well-reflected in GNL's stock price, as shares have lost more than half their value since 2015:

However, the stock has now bounced nearly 50% off the 52-week lows and appears close to breaking out of the long-running downtrend. So, is it time for this former Global Net Lease stockholder to take a second chance with this REIT? I'm not so sure; here's why I'm bearish on Global Net Lease today.

Recent Results

After stabilizing earlier in 2025, Global Net Lease stock really started to take off over the past few months. This came, in significant part, thanks to the REIT's upbeat Q3 earnings, where it slightly topped expectations. The REIT raised its full-year FFO guidance by a few cents per share. In addition, Global Net Lease recently sold its McLaren campus at what appears to be a favorable price.

While the current results appear somewhat better, I'd note that shareholders have suffered significant losses over the past few years as the company has sought to reshape its property portfolio and dispose of underperforming assets. We can see this in Global Net Lease's earnings per share figures, which have been in negative territory almost continuously since 2021:

REIT investors tend to ignore earnings per share, and to a degree, that's understandable. A REIT's dividend payments tend to be based on funds from operations rather than accounting earnings, after all.

But if a REIT is persistently running large GAAP earnings losses, that can be a hint that there is something that has gone wrong with its business model.

In Global Net Lease's case, it has been having to take considerable writedowns on its property portfolio. Here are its writedowns on a year-by-year basis:

  • 2021: $17 million
  • 2022: $22 million
  • 2023: $69 million
  • 2024: $90 million
  • Past 12 months: $146 million

These are sizable figures, and they appear to be trending in the wrong direction. To be fair, the REIT does sometimes report gains when it sells a property. However, these have been smaller in magnitude than the writedowns that it has taken on its property portfolio.

As a result of these writedowns and ensuing losses, the company's book value per share has been in an unrelenting steep decline for many years now:

The company's merger with Necessity Retail REIT and major increase in debt load and related interest payments don't appear to have done anything to arrest this slide.

Fundamentally, I just don't understand the logic in paying a premium to book value for a REIT with this sort of track record. Unless something dramatic changes, book value seems likely to keep dropping over time and take the share price down with it.

I'd also note that Global Net Lease still has about a quarter of its portfolio in office properties in particular. We've seen several office REITs go bust outright or have their shares lose the vast majority of their value in recent years.

Bulls can point to the fact that most of Global Net Lease's tenants, including those in the office segment, have high credit ratings. And that's a fair argument. I don't think Global Net Lease's tenants are at any particular risk of economic distress as compared to the rest of the REIT sector.

However, high-quality tenants don't necessarily ensure stable cash flows going forward. In the case of Global Net Lease's offices, for example, I'd argue the real risk isn't that the tenant becomes insolvent but rather that they demand major concessions on lease renewals when their existing contracts end. With so much prime office space being vacant today, current tenants have a lot of bargaining power simply by threatening to leave their present offices and move to a cheaper rival's building.

GNL Stock Verdict

I will give the REIT credit in that its operating results turned a bit more favorable toward the end of 2025. The Q3 earnings in particular were a sorely needed bright spot that finally gave the bulls some cause for real optimism. It seems less likely that Global Net Lease will need to cut the dividend again in the short run given the uptick in recent results.

Bears may say that the firm's AFFO figures seem optimistic once you reflect maintenance CAPEX and that actual core cash flows likely aren't sufficient to cover the dividend over an extended period. That said, at least for 2026, there's probably enough cushion here that the current payout can be maintained.

For a REIT that was badly out of favor, this sort of positive earnings momentum can lead to a substantial rebound in the stock price. Perhaps with another strong quarter and a potential Fed rate cut tailwind, the stock could get back above $10/share in the near term. I'd be a seller into any additional strength, however.

In the long run, this REIT's track record speaks for itself. There have been repeated dividend cuts and a steady, unrelenting erosion of the REIT's tangible book value per share. Over time, given the REIT's exposure to asset classes such as office, which have been struggling, it seems likely that the company will face more asset write-downs. While cash flow from operations currently seems to be enough to cover the dividend, I wouldn't be surprised to see another cut in coming years, especially if the economy turns downward.

I simply don't view a starting 8% dividend yield as being nearly attractive enough to warrant investing in a REIT whose cash flows and dividend payments have been declining for many years now.

As such, I rate GNL stock as a sell. I see no reason to pay a penny more than book value, at present $7.72 per share, given the REIT's checkered history and the steady erosion in the firm's asset base per share over the years.

For what it's worth, I do believe the balance sheet is strong enough that the dividends on Global Net Lease's preferred shares (GNL.PR.A) (GNL.PR.D) (GNL.PR.E) are probably safe for at least the next few years. However, with these trading near par and with yields in the seven-to-eight percent range, I don't see enough total return potential on those to be interested in buying those today, though they could be an okay "hold" for the time being for income investors.

data.title: Intel: The Selloff Was Expected, But I'm Not Turning Bullish
data.description:
I Still Maintain a Hold on Intel

In late 2025, we saw parabolic rallies among many "AI stocks" driven by continued policy support and bullish sell-side outlooks. However, their earnings have not improved yet. It's common to see stocks jump significantly higher before their growth picks up. Betting on long-term growth without current fundamental support is risky. Forward valuation multiples such as P/E become lofty as earnings decline. Sometimes, momentum trading flows can push prices beyond what fundamentals can justify.

Before the Q4 earnings release, Intel's (INTC) had rallied 180% since the August 2025 low, even though the company only maintained a flat to low-single-digit revenue growth and a fourth consecutive year of FCF declines. However, the market was betting on a successful turnaround of its data center and foundry businesses, backed by the Trump administration and external investors such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Softbank (SFTBY). The growing optimism heading into the earnings release fueled the rally, as investors believed that INTC had already passed the growth trough. However, they got a reality check after the company issued a weak Q1 FY2026 guidance.

In my last article, I maintained a Hold rating on INTC, citing execution risk given its past track record. I also noted that investments from Nvidia boosted near-term sentiment but couldn't guarantee a strong growth inflection for INTC. Competition in the foundry business has intensified, particularly from TSMC (TSM). At that time, I didn't expect any growth rebound until 2H FY2026, so a weak Q1 FY2026 outlook was expected.

The company signaled total revenue decline and flat non-GAAP EPS in the current quarter, with a big drop in gross margin and higher capex in H1. I believe INTC's previous rally was largely speculative. After a 17% pullback, the current stock price has still priced in a lot of good news. Therefore, I reiterate my Hold rating.

A Weak Start to FY2026

INTC's growth optimism faded again after Q4 FY2025 earnings, sending shares lower, which was largely expected. I see more volatility in the coming weeks given the prior relentless rally. While the company beat revenue and EPS consensus in Q4, total revenue still declined 4.1% YoY.

In addition, the 1Q FY2026 outlook reminds us that INTC's fundamentals are still weak, below consensus. It guided a 3.7% YoY revenue decline and further contraction in non-GAAP gross margin to 34.6%. The company is not expected to generate any non-GAAP EPS in Q1. During the Q4 FY2025 earnings call, management indicated that the weak Q1 outlook is driven by supply issues, and this quarter could mark the bottom of supply constraints.

They also noted "a more pronounced revenue decline in CCG than in DCAI as we continue to prioritize internal supply to our server end markets," which could imply a big drop in CCG revenue in Q1 FY2026. However, the company indicated a gradual improvement starting in Q2. Based on management's tone, I'm cautiously optimistic because we are back in a wait-and-see mode.

DCAI Is Strong, But Expect a Growth Decline in 1Q FY2026

Despite weaker overall growth, INTC's Data Center business accelerated strongly on a sequential basis throughout FY2025. Since the company integrated the Network and Edge Group (NEX) segment into CCG and DCAI in Q1 FY2025, QoQ comparisons were not "apples to apples" until Q2 FY2025. Therefore, we only have limited quarter periods to assess the DCAI growth trend, as shown on the chart above.

DCAI saw a 15% sequential growth in Q4 FY2025, but if you look at entire FY2025, the revenue was only up 5% YoY, which doesn't look strong to me. However, its revenue mix has been rising and now reached 35% of total revenue. This suggests that the demand for traditional server compute is robust. However, management also noted that "revenue would have been meaningfully higher if we had more supply."

Management expects both CCG and DCAI to be down in Q1 FY2026. One analyst in the earnings call estimated that "maybe DCAI is down high single digits and clients are down like mid-teens." Assuming a potential -7.5% QoQ for DCAI in 1Q, we can calculate the segment growth will be 6% YoY, down from 9% YoY in Q4 FY2025.

Intel Foundry Business Could Reach a Growth Inflection This Year

Intel's foundry business remained weak in FY2025. The segment revenue was only up 3% YoY. Investors believe that it will generate strong multi-year growth, starting in FY2026. The CEO said they are currently shipping the first products built on Intel 18A, in-line with expectations. This will be followed by 14A development, which could reach volume production in late 2027 or early 2028.

I previously argued that the 14A could be a critical inflection point for Intel, supported by the joint project with Nvidia. A multi-year growth acceleration would increase the revenue mix from the current 35% in FY2025, driving total revenue growth.

FCF Declines Have Stabilized, but I Still Expect a Negative Growth in FY2026

Although the company reported adjusted FCF numbers to look better by including partner contributions and proceeds from government incentives, I believe it doesn't reflect true free cash flow generation from the tradeoff between business operations and capital investments.

Over the past four years, INTC's unadjusted FCF has been declining, but the downtrend was kind of stabilized in FY2025. The company has received a lot of liquidity injections from external investors but did not generate earnings and FCF, which shows a low ROI profile. If it persists, this will lower the stock's quality.

For FY2026, management revised the capex outlook from "lower capex" to "flat or slightly lower" and indicated more capex weighted in 1H FY2026. Therefore, I expect a continued decline in unadjusted FCF until 2H.

Conclusion

In summary, Intel's Q4 FY2025 earnings didn't justify the previous strong rally, so the 17% selloff was not surprising at all. While the stock broke its last 52-week high, it failed to hold above that level after earnings. Even so, it's still up 130% in six months, which has priced in much of the growth optimism. Moreover, discussing valuation multiples is meaningless, especially trading at 90x forward P/E. The ongoing supply constraints continue to weigh on growth and margin in Q1 FY2026.

While I'm still bullish on INTC's multi-year growth rebound story, a disappointing Q1 guidance could hurt near-term sentiment, and I expect further price consolidation from here. So, I'm still neutral on INTC for now.
